title: The Universal Grammar
subtitle: An optional count and register, an operator, an optional count, then a motion or text object
summary: Vim is a tiny language: verbs (operators) compose with nouns (motions and text objects). Once you see the grammar, every key falls into place.

blocks.text: The Universal Grammar
Almost every command in Vim follows one shape:

```
[count] ["reg] operator [count] motion-or-text-object
```

Read it as a sentence: *do this thing*, optionally *with a register*, *this many times* (omitted counts default to 1), *to this stretch of text*. (Registers are named clipboards — the `"a` prefix means *register `a`*; the full treatment is in *Registers*.) Concrete examples:

rows: Three times, delete one word (same result as below)
Once, delete three words (same result as above; the counts multiply)
Change the inner word the cursor is on
Yank from cursor to end of line

blocks.text: The verbs (operators)
The nouns (motions and text objects)

Anything that moves the cursor in *normal mode* is a motion. After an operator, the same motion describes the *range* the operator acts on. So {key:w} alone moves the cursor a word forward; {key:d}{key:w} deletes the same span.

A *word* is a run of letters, digits, and underscores — any punctuation (`,`, `.`, `!`, `-`, etc.) breaks it. A *WORD* (capitalised) is anything separated by whitespace, so it may contain punctuation and symbols. For example, `foo-bar.baz` is one WORD but three words.

rows: Forward to the start of the next word
Backward to the start of the previous word
Forward to the end of this word (or the next, if already at the end)

blocks.text: Move horizontally within the current line:

rows: First non-blank character of the line
Like {key:^}, but accepts a count: {key:3}{key:_} is the first non-blank three lines down
Last non-blank character of the line

blocks.text: Move within the whole file:

rows: Jump to line number {n} (e.g. {key:4}{key:2}{key:G} for line 42)

blocks.text: Hop to a specific character without leaving the line:

rows: Forward to just before {c} (the *t* is for *till*)
Repeat the last {key:f} / {key:F} / {key:t} / {key:T} in the same direction
Repeat it in the opposite direction

blocks.text: Pattern-based motion — jump to whatever matches:

rows: Repeat the last search in its original direction
Repeat the last search in the opposite direction
Search forward for the word under the cursor
Search backward for the word under the cursor

blocks.text: Move by larger units of text:
Jump between matched pairs:

*Text objects* describe a region by its structure rather than its endpoints — the word the cursor is *in*, the parens the cursor is *between*. The prefix decides what's included: {key:i} for the *inner* contents (no delimiters), {key:a} for *a* whole object (including its delimiters and trailing whitespace). They only work after an operator or in *visual mode* — they're nouns, not motions.

rows: The word the cursor is on
A word plus its trailing whitespace
Inside a double-quoted string (excluding the quotes)
A parenthesised group, including the parens
An HTML/XML tag, including both opening and closing tags

blocks.text: The full treatment of text objects is in *Text Objects* — for now, treat them as nouns like any other.

blocks.title: Why It Composes
blocks.text:
Motions, text objects, and operators all sit behind a clean interface, and that's why every combination works.

Every motion and every text object reduces to the same shape: a *range*, written `(start, end, type)`, where *type* is `char`, `line`, or `block`. A *motion* uses the current cursor position as one endpoint and computes the other — {key:w} returns `(cursor, start-of-next-word, char)`. A *text object* uses the cursor only to locate *which* structural unit applies; both endpoints come from the structure itself — {key:i}{key:w} returns the two edges of the word containing the cursor, regardless of where within the word the cursor sits.

An *operator* takes a range and produces an effect on the buffer: delete it, yank it, change it, uppercase it.

The pieces *compose* because the output of one is exactly the input of the next — a motion or text object produces a range, an operator consumes one. Vim's source code does not branch into a special "if {key:d}, then handle {key:w}" path; it has *one* dispatch for motions and text objects, *one* dispatch for operators, and they meet through the `(start, end, type)` triple. Learn a new operator and you've simultaneously learned its combination with every motion and text object you know; learn a new text object and you've simultaneously learned its combination with every operator. The grammar scales multiplicatively.

blocks.title: The Doubled Form
blocks.text: Every operator has a *doubled* form that means "apply linewise to this line": {key:d}{key:d} deletes the line, {key:y}{key:y} yanks the line, {key:c}{key:c} changes the line, {key:>}{key:>} indents the line, {key:g}{key:U}{key:U} uppercases the line. This is a syntactic shortcut — internally, {key:d}{key:d} is processed as a special case where the motion is replaced by "the current line, linewise."

blocks.title: Three warts: {key:Y}, {key:S}, {key:s}
blocks.text:
Vim has single-key shortcuts for some of the most common operator+motion combinations. If they followed one tidy rule, an *uppercase letter* would always mean *its lowercase + {key:$}* — i.e. "apply the operator from the cursor to the end of the line". Most do; three don't, and those exceptions catch every newcomer.

• {key:D} = {key:d}{key:$}: delete from the cursor to the end of the line. *Consistent.*
• {key:C} = {key:c}{key:$}: change from the cursor to the end of the line, leaving you in *insert mode*. *Consistent.*
• {key:Y} — by the rule, *should* be {key:y}{key:$} (yank from the cursor to the end of the line). In vanilla Vim it isn't: it's aliased to {key:y}{key:y} (yank the whole current line). Neovim has finally fixed this default so {key:Y} matches the rule. *The classic wart.*
• {key:S} = {key:c}{key:c}: change the whole current line. By analogy with {key:C} you'd expect {key:c}{key:$}; it isn't. *A second wart.*
• {key:s} = {key:c}{key:l}: delete the single character under the cursor and enter *insert mode*. The grammar wouldn't predict this either. *A minor wart.*

These exceptions exist because Vi shipped before the doubled-form rule was systematic, and by the time the grammar was tidied up, fingers were already in muscle memory. The aliases stayed for backward compatibility. Treat {key:Y}, {key:S}, and {key:s} as things to memorise, not principles to derive.

When you want to learn a new key, ask: is it a *verb* (operator), a *noun* (motion / text object), or a *complete command* (like {key:x} or {key:p})? If it's the first two, you've immediately doubled the size of your vocabulary by combining it with everything you already know.
